1: The divorce controversy (19:1-12) engages the well-known debate between the schools of Shammai and Hillel on the interpretation of Deut 24:1 (the ground for divorce: some unseemly thing, erva dabar). Shammai's school restricted divorce to sexual infidelity; Hillel's school permitted divorce for any reason (even if a wife spoiled the cooking). Matthew's Jesus takes a stricter position than both schools: divorce is permitted only for porneia (sexual immorality), consistent with Shammai but grounded not in Shammai's halakic reasoning but in the creation order (Gen 1:27, 2:24).
2: Jesus' movement from Galilee to "the region of Judea beyond the Jordan" (Perean territory east of the Jordan, part of Herod Antipas's jurisdiction) follows the standard pilgrim route to Jerusalem that avoided Samaria. Large crowds following him through Perea were common at Passover season. The crowd's following and his healings establish the public context within which the Pharisees' challenge in v.3 is designed as a test before witnesses.
3: The Pharisees' question "Is it lawful to divorce one's wife for any cause?" is a technical legal probe referencing the Shammai-Hillel controversy over Deut 24:1. The phrase "for any cause" (kata pasan aitian) echoes the Hillelite interpretation: Rabbi Hillel read erwat dabar as permitting divorce for virtually any displeasure — even if a wife burned the food (m. Gittin 9:10). Rabbi Shammai restricted it to sexual immorality. Jesus is invited to declare for one school, which would alienate the other's adherents.
4: "Have you not read that he who created them from the beginning made them male and female?" — Jesus bypasses the Mosaic concession by appealing to the creation order in Gen 1:27 and 2:24. "From the beginning" (ap' archēs) is a hermeneutical move: the normative standard for marriage is not Mosaic legislation but the structure of creation itself. The Mosaic concession is explicitly framed as accommodation, not ideal.
5: The citation of Gen 2:24 ("a man shall leave his father and mother and hold fast to his wife, and they shall become one flesh") establishes the ontological foundation of marriage. "One flesh" (mia sarx) in the LXX denotes not merely sexual union but kinship solidarity — the bond that in Israelite culture carried the strongest obligation. Marriage creates this level of bond, superseding the family of origin.
6: "What God has joined together, let not man separate" — ho theos synezeuxen ("God yoked together") uses the language of a pair of animals placed under the same yoke. The passive implies divine action in each marriage. Anthrōpos mē chōrizetō: the prohibitive present imperative states a general rule. Jesus does not merely restrict divorce grounds — he reframes marriage as a divine act that human legal procedure cannot undo at its fundamental level.
7: The Pharisees counter with Moses: "Why then did Moses command one to give a certificate of divorce?" (Deut 24:1-4). The word "command" (eneteilato) is a slight misquotation — Deuteronomy 24 assumes divorce occurs and regulates its aftermath; it does not command or require a certificate. Jesus corrects this reading in v.8 by distinguishing Mosaic accommodation from Mosaic command.
8: "Because of your hardness of heart Moses allowed you to divorce your wives, but from the beginning it was not so" — sklērokardia (hardness of heart) is the LXX's standard term for Israel's covenant resistance (Ezek 3:7; Zech 7:12). Moses's permission is a concession to human moral failure, not a revelation of God's ideal. Jesus places himself in the position of announcing the Edenic order over the Mosaic accommodation — a claim of authority over the Torah's interpretation.
9: The exception clause "except for sexual immorality" (mē epi porneia) aligns Jesus closer to Shammai than Hillel but grounds the position not in halakhic reasoning but in the creation order. The word porneia has a wide semantic range including sexual immorality broadly, pre-marital unchastity, and possibly incestuous unions (cf. 1 Cor 5:1). The remarriage clause asserts that divorce does not dissolve the marriage bond at its fundamental level.
10: The disciples' response — "if such is the case with a man's wife, it is better not to marry" — reflects the radical nature of Jesus' position. In a world where divorce was a widely available male privilege (the Hillelite position was popular), removing this exit option made marriage seem prohibitively risky. Their conclusion is blunt social calculus: without easy exit, the risk of an incompatible marriage is too high.
11: "Not everyone can receive this saying, but only those to whom it is given" — chōrein (receive, contain, make room for) suggests capacity, not merely willingness. Jesus acknowledges that both celibacy and his demanding view of marriage are not universally given as vocations. The "gift" dimension of sexual ethics recurs in Paul's treatment (1 Cor 7:7), where both celibacy and marriage are designated as specific charisms.
12: Three types of eunuchs: those born so from birth (congenital conditions); those made eunuchs by others (castration performed on servants in royal courts — common in the Ptolemaic, Herodian, and Roman imperial households; Josephus describes eunuchs in Herod's court, Ant. 16.8.1); and those who have made themselves eunuchs for the kingdom (voluntary celibacy). The third type was unusual in Judaism — though the Qumran community may have included permanently celibate members. Jesus presents this as a calling, not a universal demand.
13: Children were brought to Jesus "so that he might lay hands on them and pray." Laying hands on children by elders or rabbis for blessing was practiced on Yom Kippur (documented in Soferim 18:5). The gesture invokes the patriarchal blessing tradition (Jacob blessing Ephraim and Manasseh, Gen 48:14). The disciples' rebuke reflects their misapplication of the honor logic Jesus has been overturning throughout ch. 18.
14: "Let the children come to me and do not hinder them, for to such belongs the kingdom of heaven" — tōn toioutōn ("to such") designates the structurally low-status, those without social standing to claim access. The kingdom belongs to those who arrive without leverage or inherited honor. Jesus' reception of the children — contra the disciples' gatekeeping — enacts the ch. 18 discourse concretely.
15: The laying on of hands and departure mark the completion of the blessing act. The children episode stands between the divorce discussion and the rich young ruler, framing the question of who receives kingdom access: not those with leverage or wealth, but those who arrive only in need, without social capital to claim entry.
16: "Good teacher, what good thing shall I do to inherit eternal life?" — addressing a teacher as "good" (agathe didaskale) was unusual; goodness was a divine attribute (b. Ta'anit 4a: "none is good except the Holy One"). Jesus' response invites examination of the greeting's implications. "Eternal life" (zōē aiōnios) is the eschatological inheritance of the age to come, the resurrection life of the righteous.
17: "If you want to enter life, keep the commandments" — Jesus begins with the Torah's own framework: covenantal life is bounded by covenantal obedience. The man's follow-up question ("Which ones?") suggests he expects a short list of essential commandments — a known rabbinic genre. Various teachers summarized the Torah's core: Hillel as the Golden Rule; R. Akiva as "love your neighbor as yourself"; Jesus in 22:37-40 as the double love command. Here Jesus cites the Decalogue's social commandments.
18: The commandments listed — do not murder, do not commit adultery, do not steal, do not bear false witness, honor your father and mother — are the second-table Decalogue commands governing human relationships (Exod 20:12-16; Deut 5:16-20). Jesus adds "love your neighbor as yourself" (Lev 19:18), the summary command that both Hillel and R. Akiva used to encapsulate Torah's social ethic.
19: The inclusion of Lev 19:18 alongside the Decalogue integrates the Holiness Code's social ethic with Sinai law's prohibitions. In Jewish interpretation, Lev 19:18 was understood to encompass one's whole obligation to fellow covenant members. Jesus' citation points forward to his coming demand: the one thing lacking will test whether love of neighbor extends to the poor.
20: The young man's claim — "All these I have kept. What do I still lack?" — is not dismissed as self-deception. Jewish teachers took seriously the possibility of sustained covenant faithfulness; Paul similarly claims "blameless" Torah-observance (Phil 3:6). The word "lack" (hysteroumen) is his own formulation, signaling genuine moral seriousness. Jesus' answer is not a correction of false self-assessment but a disclosure of where this man's particular attachment lies.
21: "If you want to be perfect (teleios), go, sell what you possess and give to the poor, and you will have treasure in heaven; and come, follow me." Teleios means complete, mature, fully formed — the word used in 5:48 ("Be perfect as your heavenly Father is perfect"). The call to sell everything targets this man's specific attachment, not a universal demand. "Treasure in heaven" is a well-attested Jewish concept for meritorious acts (Tobit 4:9; Sir 29:10-11; m. Peah 1:1).
22: "The young man went away sorrowful, for he had great possessions" — apēlthen lypoumenos. His grief signals genuine engagement with the call. The word "possessions" (ktēmata polla) refers to landed property — the primary form of wealth in the ancient agricultural economy, conferring not just income but social status and identity. Selling it would mean social death as well as financial loss.
23: "It is hard for a rich man to enter the kingdom of heaven" — dyskolōs (with difficulty). The statement is not absolute impossibility but a frank assessment of the structural problem wealth creates. In the patronage economy, wealth created networks of obligation, social identity, and self-sufficiency that worked against the kingdom's logic of dependence and generosity.
24: "It is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich man to enter the kingdom of God" — the image is deliberately absurd, not a reference to a narrow Jerusalem gate (that identification is a medieval legend with no first-century attestation). The Talmud uses a similar hyperbole with an elephant in contexts meaning "something impossible" (b. Berakhot 55b; b. Bava Metzia 38b). Jesus' audience would have heard deliberate comic impossibility.
25: The disciples' astonishment — "who then can be saved?" — reflects the standard assumption that wealth was a sign of divine favor (Deut 28). If the apparently blessed wealthy cannot be saved, no one can. Jesus' answer does not disagree with the premise that human effort cannot achieve salvation; it redirects toward divine possibility as the only ground.
26: "With man this is impossible, but with God all things are possible" — an echo of Gen 18:14 ("Is anything impossible for God?") in the context of Sarah's pregnancy. The allusion frames salvation as a creative act of divine power, not human achievement. The rich man's wealth becomes a category parallel to Sarah's barrenness: the obstacle is resolved not by effort but by God's power to do what is structurally impossible.
27: "We have left everything and followed you. What then will we have?" — Peter's question is honest inquiry in kingdom terms. The disciples have done what the rich young man could not. Their forsaking (aphēkamen panta) includes the same categories: home, livelihood, family networks. Jesus does not rebuke the question; reward language is present throughout the Sermon on the Mount and earlier mission discourses.
28: "In the renewal of all things" (en tē palingenesia) — a term in Stoic cosmology for the periodic regeneration of the universe, used here in an eschatological Jewish sense for the messianic renovation of creation. "The Son of Man sitting on his glorious throne" echoes Dan 7:13-14. The twelve disciples sitting on twelve thrones judging the twelve tribes of Israel positions them as eschatological regents over renewed Israel — a claim to royal function in the coming age.
29: "Everyone who has left houses or brothers or sisters or father or mother or children or fields for my name's sake will receive a hundredfold" — the list is identical to the categories of kinship and property that bound a person into the ancient household economy. To leave them "for my name's sake" substitutes Jesus as the primary loyalty-object replacing family and land. The hundredfold return and eternal life are the kingdom's eschatological economy.
30: "But many who are first will be last, and the last first" — the honor-reversal principle structuring chs. 18-19 (children, eunuchs, the poor) is restated as a general eschatological law. The parable of the laborers in ch. 20:1-16 will immediately illustrate this reversal with the equal payment of those hired last and first.
